## DENOISING DIFFUSION IMPLICIT MODELS

## **Jiaming Song, Chenlin Meng & Stefano Ermon**

Stanford University

_{_ tsong,chenlin,ermon _}_ @cs.stanford.edu

## ABSTRACT

Denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) have achieved high quality image generation without adversarial training, yet they require simulating a Markov chain for many steps in order to produce a sample. To accelerate sampling, we present denoising diffusion implicit models (DDIMs), a more efficient class of iterative implicit probabilistic models with the same training procedure as DDPMs. In DDPMs, the generative process is defined as the reverse of a particular Markovian diffusion process. We generalize DDPMs via a class of non-Markovian diffusion processes that lead to the same training objective. These non-Markovian processes can correspond to generative processes that are deterministic, giving rise to implicit models that produce high quality samples much faster. We empirically demonstrate that DDIMs can produce high quality samples 10 _×_ to 50 _×_ faster in terms of wall-clock time compared to DDPMs, allow us to trade off computation for sample quality, perform semantically meaningful image interpolation directly in the latent space, and reconstruct observations with very low error.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Deep generative models have demonstrated the ability to produce high quality samples in many domains (Karras et al., 2020; van den Oord et al., 2016a). In terms of image generation, generative adversarial networks (GANs, Goodfellow et al. (2014)) currently exhibits higher sample quality than likelihood-based methods such as variational autoencoders (Kingma & Welling, 2013), autoregressive models (van den Oord et al., 2016b) and normalizing flows (Rezende & Mohamed, 2015; Dinh et al., 2016). However, GANs require very specific choices in optimization and architectures in order to stabilize training (Arjovsky et al., 2017; Gulrajani et al., 2017; Karras et al., 2018; Brock et al., 2018), and could fail to cover modes of the data distribution (Zhao et al., 2018).

Recent works on iterative generative models (Bengio et al., 2014), such as denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPM, Ho et al. (2020)) and noise conditional score networks (NCSN, Song & Ermon (2019)) have demonstrated the ability to produce samples comparable to that of GANs, without having to perform adversarial training. To achieve this, many denoising autoencoding models are trained to denoise samples corrupted by various levels of Gaussian noise. Samples are then produced by a Markov chain which, starting from white noise, progressively denoises it into an image. This generative Markov Chain process is either based on Langevin dynamics (Song & Ermon, 2019) or obtained by reversing a forward _diffusion process_ that progressively turns an image into noise (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015).

A critical drawback of these models is that they require many iterations to produce a high quality sample. For DDPMs, this is because that the generative process (from noise to data) approximates the reverse of the forward _diffusion process_ (from data to noise), which could have thousands of steps; iterating over all the steps is required to produce a single sample, which is much slower compared to GANs, which only needs one pass through a network. For example, it takes around 20 hours to sample 50k images of size 32 _×_ 32 from a DDPM, but less than a minute to do so from a GAN on a Nvidia 2080 Ti GPU. This becomes more problematic for larger images as sampling 50k images of size 256 _×_ 256 could take nearly 1000 hours on the same GPU.

To close this efficiency gap between DDPMs and GANs, we present denoising diffusion implicit models (DDIMs). DDIMs are implicit probabilistic models (Mohamed & Lakshminarayanan, 2016) and are closely related to DDPMs, in the sense that they are trained with the same objective function.

In Section 3, we generalize the forward _diffusion process_ used by DDPMs, which is Markovian, to _non-Markovian_ ones, for which we are still able to design suitable reverse generative Markov chains. We show that the resulting variational training objectives have a shared surrogate objective, which is _exactly_ the objective used to train DDPM. Therefore, we can freely choose from a large family of generative models using the same neural network simply by choosing a different, _nonMarkovian_ diffusion process (Section 4.1) and the corresponding reverse generative Markov Chain. In particular, we are able to use _non-Markovian_ diffusion processes which lead to ”short” generative Markov chains (Section 4.2) that can be simulated in a small number of steps. This can massively increase sample efficiency only at a minor cost in sample quality.

In Section 5, we demonstrate several empirical benefits of DDIMs over DDPMs. _First_ , DDIMs have superior sample generation quality compared to DDPMs, when we accelerate sampling by 10 _×_ to 100 _×_ using our proposed method. _Second_ , DDIM samples have the following “consistency” property, which does not hold for DDPMs: if we start with the same initial latent variable and generate several samples with Markov chains of various lengths, these samples would have similar high-level features. _Third_ , because of “consistency” in DDIMs, we can perform semantically meaningful image interpolation by manipulating the initial latent variable in DDIMs, unlike DDPMs which interpolates near the image space due to the stochastic generative process.

## 2 BACKGROUND

Given samples from a data distribution _q_ ( _**x**_ 0), we are interested in learning a model distribution _pθ_ ( _**x**_ 0) that approximates _q_ ( _**x**_ 0) and is easy to sample from. Unlike typical latent variable models (such as the variational autoencoder (Rezende et al., 2014)), DDPMs are learned with a fixed (rather than trainable) inference procedure _q_ ( _**x**_ 1: _T |_ _**x**_ 0), and latent variables are relatively high dimensional. This is called the _forward process_ due to the autoregressive nature of the sampling procedure (from _**x**_ 0 to _**x** T_ ). We call the latent variable model _pθ_ ( _**x**_ 0: _T_ ), which is a Markov chain that samples from _**x** T_ to _**x**_ 0, the _generative process_ , since it approximates the intractable _reverse process q_ ( _**x** t−_ 1 _|_ _**x** t_ ). Intuitively, the forward process progressively adds noise to the observation _**x**_ 0, whereas the generative process progressively denoises a noisy observation (Figure 1, left).

(2020), the objective with _γ_ = **1** is optimized instead to maximize generation performance of the trained model; this is also the same objective used in noise conditional score networks (Song & Ermon, 2019) based on score matching (Hyv¨arinen, 2005; Vincent, 2011). From a trained model, _**x**_ 0 is sampled by first sampling _**x** T_ from the prior _pθ_ ( _**x** T_ ), and then sampling _**x** t−_ 1 from the generative processes iteratively.

The length _T_ of the forward process is an important hyperparameter in DDPMs. From a variational perspective, a large _T_ allows the reverse process to be close to a Gaussian (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015), so that the generative process modeled with Gaussian conditional distributions becomes a good approximation; this motivates the choice of large _T_ values, such as _T_ = 1000 in Ho et al. (2020). However, as all _T_ iterations have to be performed sequentially, instead of in parallel, to obtain a sample _**x**_ 0, sampling from DDPMs is much slower than sampling from other deep generative models, which makes them impractical for tasks where compute is limited and latency is critical.

## 3 VARIATIONAL INFERENCE FOR NON-MARKOVIAN FORWARD PROCESSES

Because the generative model approximates the reverse of the inference process, we need to rethink the inference process in order to reduce the number of iterations required by the generative model. Our key observation is that the DDPM objective in the form of _Lγ_ only depends on the marginals[2] _q_ ( _**x** t|_ _**x**_ 0), but not directly on the joint _q_ ( _**x**_ 1: _T |_ _**x**_ 0). Since there are many inference distributions (joints) with the same marginals, we explore alternative inference processes that are non-Markovian, which leads to new generative processes (Figure 1, right). These non-Markovian inference process lead to the same surrogate objective function as DDPM, as we will show below. In Appendix A, we show that the non-Markovian perspective also applies beyond the Gaussian case.

## 4 SAMPLING FROM GENERALIZED GENERATIVE PROCESSES

With _L_ **1** as the objective, we are not only learning a generative process for the Markovian inference process considered in Sohl-Dickstein et al. (2015) and Ho et al. (2020), but also generative processes for many non-Markovian forward processes parametrized by _σ_ that we have described. Therefore, we can essentially use pretrained DDPM models as the solutions to the new objectives, and focus on finding a generative process that is better at producing samples subject to our needs by changing _σ_ .

We note another special case when _σt_ = 0 for all _t_[5] ; the forward process becomes deterministic given _**x** t−_ 1 and _**x**_ 0, except for _t_ = 1; in the generative process, the coefficient before the random noise _ϵt_ becomes zero. The resulting model becomes an implicit probabilistic model (Mohamed & Lakshminarayanan, 2016), where samples are generated from latent variables with a fixed procedure (from _**x** T_ to _**x**_ 0). We name this the _denoising diffusion implicit model_ (DDIM, pronounced /d:Im/), because it is an implicit probabilistic model trained with the DDPM objective (despite the forward process no longer being a diffusion).

## 4.2 ACCELERATED GENERATION PROCESSES

In the previous sections, the generative process is considered as the approximation to the reverse process; since of the forward process has _T_ steps, the generative process is also forced to sample _T_ steps. However, as the denoising objective _L_ **1** does not depend on the specific forward procedure as long as _qσ_ ( _**x** t|_ _**x**_ 0) is fixed, we may also consider forward processes with lengths smaller than _T_ , which accelerates the corresponding generative processes without having to train a different model.

Let us consider the forward process as defined not on all the latent variables _**x**_ 1: _T_ , but on a subset _{_ _**x** τ_ 1 _, . . . ,_ _**x** τS }_ , where _τ_ is an increasing sub-sequence of [1 _, . . . , T_ ] of length _S_ . In particular, we define the sequential forward process over _**x** τ_ 1 _, . . . ,_ _**x** τS_ such that _q_ ( _**x** τi|_ _**x**_ 0) = _N_ ( _[√] ατi_ _**x**_ 0 _,_ (1 _− ατi_ ) _**I**_ ) matches the “marginals” (see Figure 2 for an illustration). The generative process now samples latent variables according to reversed( _τ_ ), which we term _(sampling) trajectory_ . When the length of the sampling trajectory is much smaller than _T_ , we may achieve significant increases in computational efficiency due to the iterative nature of the sampling process.

Using a similar argument as in Section 3, we can justify using the model trained with the _L_ **1** objective, so no changes are needed in training. We show that only slight changes to the updates in Eq. (12) are needed to obtain the new, faster generative processes, which applies to DDPM, DDIM, as well as all generative processes considered in Eq. (10). We include these details in Appendix C.1.

In principle, this means that we can train a model with an arbitrary number of forward steps but only sample from some of them in the generative process. Therefore, the trained model could consider many more steps than what is considered in (Ho et al., 2020) or even a continuous time variable _t_ (Chen et al., 2020). We leave empirical investigations of this aspect as future work.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

In this section, we show that DDIMs outperform DDPMs in terms of image generation when fewer iterations are considered, giving speed ups of 10 _×_ to 100 _×_ over the original DDPM generation process. Moreover, unlike DDPMs, once the initial latent variables _**x** T_ are fixed, DDIMs retain highlevel image features regardless of the generation trajectory, so they are able to perform interpolation directly from the latent space. DDIMs can also be used to encode samples that reconstruct them from the latent code, which DDPMs cannot do due to the stochastic sampling process.

For each dataset, we use the **same trained model** with _T_ = 1000 and the objective being _Lγ_ from Eq. (5) with _γ_ = **1** ; as we argued in Section 3, no changes are needed with regards to the training procedure. The only changes that we make is **how we produce samples from the model** ; we achieve this by controlling _τ_ (which controls how fast the samples are obtained) and _σ_ (which interpolates between the deterministic DDIM and the stochastic DDPM).

## 5.1 SAMPLE QUALITY AND EFFICIENCY

(2020) where DDIM is marginally worse. However, the sample quality of _σ_ ˆ becomes much worse for smaller dim( _τ_ ), which suggests that it is ill-suited for shorter trajectories. DDIM, on the other hand, achieves high sample quality much more consistently.

Even though DDPM could also achieve reasonable sample quality with 100 _×_ steps, DDIM requires much fewer steps to achieve this; on CelebA, the FID score of the 100 step DDPM is similar to that of the 20 step DDIM.

## 5.2 SAMPLE CONSISTENCY IN DDIMS

For DDIM, the generative process is deterministic, and _**x**_ 0 would depend only on the initial state _**x** T_ . In Figure 5, we observe the generated images under different generative trajectories (i.e. different _τ_ ) while starting with the same initial _**x** T_ . Interestingly, for the generated images with the same initial _**x** T_ , most high-level features are similar, regardless of the generative trajectory. In many cases, samples generated with only 20 steps are already very similar to ones generated with 1000 steps in terms of high-level features, with only minor differences in details. Therefore, it would appear that _**x** T_ alone would be an informative latent encoding of the image; and minor details that affects sample quality are encoded in the parameters, as longer sample trajectories gives better quality samples but do not significantly affect the high-level features. We show more samples in Appendix D.4.

- 5.3 INTERPOLATION IN DETERMINISTIC GENERATIVE PROCESSES

Figure 6: Interpolation of samples from DDIM with dim( _τ_ ) = 50.

Since the high level features of the DDIM sample is encoded by _**x** T_ , we are interested to see whether it would exhibit the semantic interpolation effect similar to that observed in other implicit probabilistic models, such as GANs (Goodfellow et al., 2014). This is different from the interpolation procedure in Ho et al. (2020), since in DDPM the same _**x** T_ would lead to highly diverse _**x**_ 0 due to the stochastic generative process[6] . In Figure 6, we show that simple interpolations in _**x** T_ can lead to semantically meaningful interpolations between two samples. We include more details and samples in Appendix D.5. This allows DDIM to control the generated images on a high level directly through the latent variables, which DDPMs cannot.

## 6 RELATED WORK

Our work is based on a large family of existing methods on learning generative models as transition operators of Markov chains (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015; Bengio et al., 2014; Salimans et al., 2014; Song et al., 2017; Goyal et al., 2017; Levy et al., 2017). Among them, denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs, Ho et al. (2020)) and noise conditional score networks (NCSN, Song & Ermon (2019; 2020)) have recently achieved high sample quality comparable to GANs (Brock et al., 2018; Karras et al., 2018). DDPMs optimize a variational lower bound to the log-likelihood, whereas NCSNs optimize the score matching objective (Hyv¨arinen, 2005) over a nonparametric Parzen density estimator of the data (Vincent, 2011; Raphan & Simoncelli, 2011).

Despite their different motivations, DDPMs and NCSNs are closely related. Both use a denoising autoencoder objective for many noise levels, and both use a procedure similar to Langevin dynamics to produce samples (Neal et al., 2011). Since Langevin dynamics is a discretization of a gradient flow (Jordan et al., 1998), both DDPM and NCSN require many steps to achieve good sample quality. This aligns with the observation that DDPM and existing NCSN methods have trouble generating high-quality samples in a few iterations.

DDIM, on the other hand, is an implicit generative model (Mohamed & Lakshminarayanan, 2016) where samples are uniquely determined from the latent variables. Hence, DDIM has certain properties that resemble GANs (Goodfellow et al., 2014) and invertible flows (Dinh et al., 2016), such as the ability to produce semantically meaningful interpolations. We derive DDIM from a purely variational perspective, where the restrictions of Langevin dynamics are not relevant; this could partially explain why we are able to observe superior sample quality compared to DDPM under fewer iterations. The sampling procedure of DDIM is also reminiscent of neural networks with continuous depth (Chen et al., 2018; Grathwohl et al., 2018), since the samples it produces from the same latent variable have similar high-level visual features, regardless of the specific sample trajectory.

## 7 DISCUSSION

We have presented DDIMs – an implicit generative model trained with denoising auto-encoding / score matching objectives – from a purely variational perspective. DDIM is able to generate highquality samples much more efficiently than existing DDPMs and NCSNs, with the ability to perform meaningful interpolations from the latent space. The non-Markovian forward process presented here seems to suggest continuous forward processes other than Gaussian (which cannot be done in the original diffusion framework, since Gaussian is the only stable distribution with finite variance). We also demonstrated a discrete case with a multinomial forward process in Appendix A, and it would be interesting to investigate similar alternatives for other combinatorial structures.

Moreover, since the sampling procedure of DDIMs is similar to that of an neural ODE, it would be interesting to see if methods that decrease the discretization error in ODEs, including multistep methods such as Adams-Bashforth (Butcher & Goodwin, 2008), could be helpful for further improving sample quality in fewer steps (Queiruga et al., 2020). It is also relevant to investigate whether DDIMs exhibit other properties of existing implicit models (Bau et al., 2019).
> 7 Since _**x** T_ and _**x**_ 0 have the same dimensions, their compression qualities are not our immediate concern.

coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3-flash-preview)
coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIMs), a class of non-Markovian generative models that share the same training objective as standard DDPMs but allow for deterministic sampling and significantly fewer inference steps. By generalizing the forward process, the authors demonstrate that one can use pre-trained diffusion models to produce high-quality samples in 10-50x fewer steps while enabling latent space interpolation and reconstruction.

The paper provides a significant advancement in the efficiency of diffusion models, addressing the primary bottleneck of slow sampling. The derivation of a unified variational objective for non-Markovian processes is mathematically elegant and practically impactful, as it requires no retraining of existing models. The empirical results on CIFAR-10 and CelebA clearly demonstrate a new state-of-the-art in the sampling speed-to-quality tradeoff.

Overview issues:

- Discretization Error in the ODE Mapping: Section 4.3 characterizes DDIM sampling as an Euler integration of a specific ODE. However, the paper lacks a rigorous analysis of the discretization error introduced when the number of steps S is significantly smaller than the training steps T. While Table 1 shows empirical FID scores, there is no theoretical bound on how the error in the predicted noise epsilon_theta propagates through the deterministic trajectory compared to the stochastic one. This is particularly important because deterministic paths may accumulate bias more severely than stochastic ones where noise can act as a regularizer. The authors should include a brief analysis or citation regarding the local truncation error of the proposed update rule in Equation 13 to justify why Euler integration remains stable at very low step counts like S=10.

- Assumption of Optimal Noise Schedules for Accelerated Sampling: The methodology in Section 4.2 assumes that a noise schedule (alpha_t) optimized for a 1000-step Markovian process is also suitable for a 20-step non-Markovian trajectory. In standard DDPMs, the schedule is designed to ensure the reverse transition is approximately Gaussian. When skipping steps, the 'jump' in variance between tau_i and tau_{i-1} becomes much larger, potentially violating the local linearity assumed by the model. The paper would be strengthened by an experiment or discussion on whether the alpha schedule should be re-scaled or modified when the sampling trajectory is known to be short, as the current results might be sub-optimal for the accelerated regime.

- Limited Evaluation of Sample Diversity: A known risk of deterministic sampling (eta=0) in implicit models is the potential for mode collapse or reduced sample diversity compared to stochastic Langevin-like dynamics. While Section 5.1 focuses on FID, which captures both quality and diversity to an extent, it does not explicitly measure the 'variety' of the generated images relative to the original DDPM. Since the paper claims DDIMs are implicit probabilistic models (Section 4.1), it is vital to confirm that the deterministic mapping from the Gaussian prior to the data manifold does not truncate the support of the distribution. Adding a metric like Recall or Precision-and-Recall for Generative Models would clarify if the speedup comes at the cost of covering fewer modes of the data.

- Reconstruction Fidelity vs. Sampling Steps: Section 5.4 and Table 2 discuss reconstruction from latent space, noting that error decreases as S increases. However, the paper does not address the practical utility of this reconstruction for downstream tasks at the accelerated speeds (e.g., S=20 or 50) emphasized in the introduction. At S=10, the error is 0.014, which is significantly higher than at S=1000. For DDIM to be truly useful as an invertible encoder, the authors should define a threshold of 'perceptual' reconstruction and discuss whether the accelerated trajectories maintain semantic identity or merely a coarse approximation of the input image.

- Surrogate Objective Weights and Model Optimality: Theorem 1 establishes that the non-Markovian objective J_sigma is equivalent to the DDPM objective L_gamma for some weights gamma. However, the paper uses a model trained with gamma=1 (the simple objective from Ho et al. 2020). There is a missing discussion on whether the optimal epsilon_theta for the DDPM objective is truly the optimal epsilon_theta for the DDIM objective when the weights gamma implied by Theorem 1 deviate from 1. If the weights are significantly different, the pre-trained weights might be systematically biased for the deterministic generative process, even if they are 'good enough' to produce the reported FID scores.

- Missing comparison with contemporary fast-sampling baselines: The paper positions DDIM as a solution to the slow sampling of DDPMs but primarily compares against the original 1000-step DDPM and its own accelerated variants. At the time of ICLR 2021, other methods for accelerating diffusion or score-based models, such as the Langevin-based acceleration in NCSNv2 or early distillation techniques, were relevant. Readers cannot determine if DDIM's performance is state-of-the-art for fast sampling without a direct comparison to at least one other contemporary acceleration technique on the CIFAR-10 FID benchmark. Adding a comparison to the fast sampling results from Song & Ermon (2020) would provide the necessary context to evaluate the magnitude of the contribution.

- Lack of demonstration on high-resolution datasets: The paper claims that sampling becomes more problematic for larger images, citing 256x256 as a specific pain point in the introduction. However, the quantitative results in Table 1 and Table 2 are limited to CIFAR-10 (32x32) and CelebA (64x64). It is not clear if the 'consistency' property or the FID advantages of the deterministic mapping hold when the pixel space is significantly larger and the manifold is more complex. To fulfill the promise of the introduction, the authors should include FID results or at least qualitative samples for a higher-resolution dataset like LSUN Church or CelebA-HQ 256x256 using the S=20 and S=50 trajectories.

- Incomplete discussion of the ODE encoding-decoding cycle: Section 5.4 introduces the ability to encode an image into noise and reconstruct it, yet it only reports Mean Squared Error (MSE). In the context of generative modeling, MSE is often a poor proxy for semantic preservation. The paper lacks a visual demonstration of the reconstruction cycle—taking a real image, encoding it to x_T via the ODE, and decoding it back—to show whether the 'low error' reported in Table 2 translates to a perceptually identical image. Providing a figure that shows the original image, the x_T latent (as a noise map), and the reconstruction for S=50 would demonstrate that the ODE mapping is practically useful for image manipulation.

Recommendation: accept

Revision targets:

- Include a discussion or error analysis on the discretization of the ODE in Section 4.3, specifically addressing why Euler integration is sufficient for very small S.

- Add diversity metrics (such as Recall) to Table 1 to ensure that deterministic sampling does not significantly reduce mode coverage compared to DDPM.

- Clarify the relationship between the weights gamma in Theorem 1 and the uniform weights used in training, discussing potential sub-optimality.

- Provide a visual comparison of reconstruction quality at S=20 versus S=1000 to qualitatively validate the MSE numbers in Table 2.

comments.quote:
n reparameterize $(\sqrt{1-\alpha}/\sqrt{\alpha})$ with $\lambda$ and $(\bm{x}/\sqrt{\alpha})$ with $H(\lambda)$ then sampling $\bm{x}_{0}$ with Equation (13) can be treated as integration over the following ODE:

$H(0)=\int_{M}^{0}\epsilon_{\theta}^{\lambda}(H(\lambda)\sqrt{\lambda^{2}-1})\mathrm{d}\lambda+H(M),\quad
comments.explanation: The argument of the noise prediction network in Equation 14 is mathematically inconsistent with your stated reparameterization. If $\lambda = \sqrt{1-\alpha}/\sqrt{\alpha}$, then $\lambda^2 = (1-\alpha)/\alpha$, which implies $1/\alpha = \lambda^2 + 1$. Consequently, $\bm{x} = H(\lambda)\sqrt{\alpha} = H(\lambda)/\sqrt{\lambda^2 + 1}$. The integrand in Eq. 14 uses $H(\lambda)\sqrt{\lambda^2 - 1}$ instead. Under your definition of $\lambda$, the term $\sqrt{\lambda^2 - 1}$ would be $\sqrt{(1-2\alpha)/\alpha}$, which does not match the required scaling factor for the input $\bm{x}$. Furthermore, for diffusion schedules where $\alpha > 0.5$, $\lambda^2 - 1$ becomes negative, making the square root undefined for real numbers. The argument of $\epsilon_{\theta}$ should be $H(\lambda)/\sqrt{\lambda^2 + 1}$.

comments.title: Incorrect Integration Limits for Latent Variable H
comments.quote: $H(0)=\int_{M}^{0}\epsilon_{\theta}^{\lambda}(H(\lambda)\sqrt{\lambda^{2}-1})\mathrm{d}\lambda+H(M),\quad H(M)\sim\mathcal{N}(\bm{0},\bm{I})$
comments.explanation: The initial condition for $H$ in Equation 14 contradicts the definition of the diffusion process. You define $H(\lambda) = \bm{x}/\sqrt{\alpha}$. At the start of the generative process ($t=T$), $\alpha_T \approx 0$, which implies $\lambda \to \infty$. Since $H(M) = \bm{x}_M / \sqrt{\alpha_M}$ and $\bm{x}_M \sim \mathcal{N}(\bm{0}, \bm{I})$, the variance of $H(M)$ must be $1/\alpha_M$. As $\alpha_M \to 0$, the variance of $H(M)$ diverges, meaning $H(M)$ cannot be distributed as a standard normal $\mathcal{N}(\bm{0}, \bm{I})$. The standard normal distribution applies to $\bm{x}_T$, not the scaled variable $H$.

comments.title: Unspecified Constraint on Sigma to Ensure Real-Valued Mean
comments.quote: $q_{\sigma}(\bm{x}_{t-1}|\bm{x}_{t},\bm{x}_{0})=\mathcal{N}\left(\sqrt{\alpha_{t-1}}\bm{x}_{0}+\sqrt{1-\alpha_{t-1}-\sigma_{t}^{2}}\cdot\frac{\bm{x}_{t}-\sqrt{\alpha_{t}}\bm{x}_{0}}{\sqrt{1-\alpha_{t}}},\sigma_{t}^{2}\bm{I}\right).$
comments.explanation: The mean of the conditional distribution in Equation 7 contains the term $\sqrt{1-\alpha_{t-1}-\sigma_{t}^{2}}$. For this to be a valid real-valued Gaussian distribution, the radicand must be non-negative, implying $\sigma_t^2 \leq 1 - \alpha_{t-1}$. While the paper later discusses specific values for $\sigma$ (like the DDIM limit), the general definition of the family $\mathcal{Q}$ is incomplete without this constraint. If $\sigma_t^2 > 1 - \alpha_{t-1}$, the mean becomes complex-valued. You should explicitly state the condition $\sigma_t^2 \leq 1 - \alpha_{t-1}$ for all $t$.

comments.title: Inconsistency in variance definition for DDPM equivalence
comments.quote:
$\sigma_{\tau_{i}}(\eta)=\eta\sqrt{(1-\alpha_{\tau_{i-1}})/(1-\alpha_{\tau_{i}})}\sqrt{1-\alpha_{\tau_{i}}/\alpha_{\tau_{i-1}}},$ (15)

where $\eta\in\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ is a hyperparameter that we can directly control. This includes an original DDPM generative process when $\eta=1$
comments.explanation: The claim that $\eta=1$ corresponds to the original DDPM generative process is mathematically inconsistent when skipping steps. In the original DDPM, the variance is defined relative to the Markovian forward process of $T$ steps. While Equation 15 reduces to the posterior variance $\tilde{\beta}_t$ when $\tau$ is the full sequence, it does not represent the standard DDPM transition when $\tau$ is a sub-sequence because the Markovian property is broken. For a sub-sequence, the 'equivalent' stochastic process requires a different variance to match the marginals correctly. Clarify that $\eta=1$ corresponds to a stochastic variant of the non-Markovian process that matches DDPM marginals, rather than the original DDPM Markov chain itself.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim regarding parameter encoding of image details
comments.quote: Therefore, it would appear that  $\pmb{x}_T$  alone would be an informative latent encoding of the image; and minor details that affects sample quality are encoded in the parameters
comments.explanation: The claim that minor details are 'encoded in the parameters' rather than the latent variable is logically inconsistent with the deterministic nature of the DDIM ODE. In a deterministic mapping, the final image is a function of the initial state and the fixed model parameters. If the image changes as the number of steps increases, it is a result of reduced discretization error in the numerical integration, not a change in where information is stored. The parameters cannot 'encode' specific details of one image over another in a way that varies with trajectory length. These differences are better described as the convergence of the numerical solver toward the true solution of the probability flow ODE.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces DDIM, a significant advancement in diffusion model sampling that enables orders-of-magnitude faster generation without retraining. It successfully reinterprets the DDPM framework through a non-Markovian lens, deriving a family of sampling processes that include deterministic variants. The work is foundational, well-motivated, and has already inspired numerous follow-ups in fast sampling and latent space manipulation. Its theoretical clarity and empirical validation on standard benchmarks establish it as a major contribution to generative modeling.

Overview issues:

- Deterministic sampling claim lacks formal justification: The paper asserts that setting $\sigma_t = 0$ yields a deterministic generative process (DDIM), but this is not formally proven. Equation (12) shows that the noise term $\sigma_t \epsilon_t$ vanishes when $\sigma_t = 0$, yet the process still depends on the stochasticity of $\epsilon_\theta^{(t)}(\bm{x}_t)$, which is a learned function of noisy inputs. Unless the score model $\epsilon_\theta$ is itself deterministic and perfectly calibrated—which is not guaranteed—the generative process may retain stochasticity due to model uncertainty or approximation error. This undermines the claim of full determinism and affects downstream properties like exact reconstruction and interpolation. The authors should either prove that the process is deterministic under typical training conditions or qualify their claims about determinism based on empirical variance measurements across sampling runs with fixed $\bm{x}_T$.

- Inconsistency in reconstruction error reporting: Table 2 reports reconstruction errors decreasing with more steps (from 0.014 at $S=10$ to 0.0001 at $S=1000$), suggesting better reconstructions with longer trajectories. However, the text claims that "DDIMs have lower reconstruction error for larger $S$ values" as if this were a desirable property, while the goal of accelerated sampling is to achieve good reconstructions with small $S$. A high reconstruction error at low $S$ contradicts the efficiency narrative. Moreover, the paper does not compare these errors to DDPM (which cannot reconstruct at all), nor does it clarify whether the encoding path (reverse ODE) uses the same $\tau$ schedule as decoding. This omission makes it unclear whether the reported reconstructions are practically useful or merely reflect overfitting to long trajectories. The authors should report reconstruction fidelity at low $S$ (e.g., $S=20$) and explicitly discuss the trade-off between speed and reconstruction accuracy.

- Sampling acceleration conflates trajectory length with computational cost: The paper claims 10$\times$ to 50$\times$ speedups based on reducing $S = |\tau|$ from 1000 to 20–100, but Figure 4 shows nearly linear scaling of wall-clock time with $S$, implying that each step in $\tau$ costs the same as a full DDPM step. However, if $\tau$ skips intermediate network evaluations (i.e., uses a coarser schedule), then the speedup depends on how $\epsilon_\theta^{(t)}$ is evaluated at arbitrary $t$. The model was trained on fixed $\alpha_t$ schedules with $T=1000$, so evaluating it at arbitrary $\tau_i$ requires interpolation or retraining—neither of which is addressed. Without evidence that the network generalizes well to unseen noise levels, the reported speedups may not hold in practice. The authors should clarify whether their implementation reuses the original network without modification and validate that score predictions at skipped steps are accurate.

- Missing comparison to concurrent fast sampling methods: The paper positions DDIM as a breakthrough in sampling efficiency but only compares against DDPM variants (including $\hat\sigma$) and does not benchmark against other known fast sampling techniques available at the time, such as knowledge distillation (e.g., Progressive Distillation, 2021) or ancestral sampling improvements. Given that the literature review acknowledges prior work on score-based models and ODE solvers, omitting comparisons to alternative acceleration strategies weakens the empirical contribution. Readers cannot assess whether the gains come from the implicit formulation or simply from using fewer steps. The authors should include a comparison to at least one contemporary fast sampler under identical architectural and training conditions to isolate the benefit of the DDIM framework.

- Overstatement of interpolation semantics without user study or metric: The paper claims that DDIM enables "semantically meaningful interpolation" (Section 5.3, Figure 6), but this is supported only by qualitative examples. No quantitative evaluation—such as linear separability of interpolated attributes, consistency in classifier predictions, or human evaluation—is provided. In contrast, GANs have established protocols for assessing interpolation quality (e.g., linear disentanglement, endpoint fidelity). Without such validation, the claim risks being subjective. Furthermore, the interpolation is performed in $\bm{x}_T \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)$, which is unstructured; there is no evidence that the latent space is meaningfully organized beyond smooth transitions. The authors should either introduce a metric for semantic coherence or temper their claims to reflect that the interpolation appears visually smooth but may not align with semantic factors.

- No ablation on noise schedule design: The paper claims that DDIM enables fast sampling by reusing a DDPM-trained model with arbitrary subsets of the noise schedule, yet it does not evaluate how the choice of schedule (e.g., linear, cosine, log-spaced) affects performance under acceleration. In diffusion models, the noise schedule critically influences both training stability and sampling dynamics. A natural benchmark—such as comparing geometric, linear, or learned schedules—would show whether DDIM's efficiency gains are robust across schedule types or depend on specific $	au$ constructions. The authors should compute FID versus step count for at least two distinct noise schedules (e.g., linear vs. cosine) on CIFAR-10 and report the results in a new table or figure to validate generalizability.

- Missing comparison to distillation-based sampling: The paper claims 10× to 50× speedups but omits comparison to knowledge distillation methods like Progressive Distillation, which achieve similar acceleration by training a student model to sample in few steps. Since both approaches aim to reduce sampling cost while preserving quality, readers cannot assess whether DDIM's gains are due to its implicit formulation or simply stem from using fewer steps with the same network. The authors should compare DDIM ($	au=20$, $	au=50$) against a distilled model on the same architecture and dataset (e.g., CelebA), reporting FID and sample diversity metrics under identical conditions to isolate the contribution of the non-Markovian process.

- No evaluation of latent space coherence: The paper claims that DDIM enables semantic interpolation in latent space because $ oldsymbol{x}_T$ encodes high-level features, but it does not test whether this structure supports meaningful manipulation. For example, standard tests like linear separability of class labels in $ oldsymbol{x}_T$ or direction-based attribute editing (e.g., smile vector) would support the claim. Without such analysis, the interpolation may reflect smoothness rather than semantics. The authors should train a linear classifier on $ oldsymbol{x}_T$ to predict image attributes (e.g., gender, smile) and show that interpolations traverse meaningful directions in this space, as is standard in GAN latent space analysis.

- Deterministic consistency not quantified: The paper introduces a 'consistency' property: samples from the same $ oldsymbol{x}_T$ retain high-level features across different step counts. However, this is only shown qualitatively in Figure 5. To establish this as a measurable advantage, the authors should quantify consistency using a perceptual similarity metric (e.g., LPIPS) between pairs of samples generated from the same $ oldsymbol{x}_T$ but different $	au$. Comparing this to DDPM (with multiple stochastic runs) would show whether DDIM’s consistency is statistically significant. Without such measurements, the claim remains anecdotal.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide theoretical or empirical justification for the determinism of DDIM when $\sigma_t = 0$, including variance analysis across multiple sampling runs with fixed $\bm{x}_T$.

- Clarify the relationship between trajectory length $S$ and actual computational cost, and validate that the score network can be evaluated at arbitrary $t$ without performance degradation.

- Include quantitative evaluation of interpolation quality or revise claims about semantic meaning to reflect the lack of objective validation.

- Report reconstruction error at low $S$ and discuss its implications for practical usability in encoding-decoding tasks.

- Add comparisons to at least one contemporary fast sampling method (e.g., distillation-based) under matched conditions to contextualize the efficiency gains.

comments.title: Incorrect expansion of variational objective in Equation (11)
comments.quote:

$J_{\sigma}(\epsilon_{\theta}):=\mathbb{E}_{\bm{x}_{0:T}\sim q_{\sigma}(\bm{x}_{0:T})}[\log q_{\sigma}(\bm{x}_{1:T}|\bm{x}_{0})-\log p_{\theta}(\bm{x}_{0:T})]$ (11)
$=\mathbb{E}_{\bm{x}_{0:T}\sim q_{\sigma}(\bm{x}_{0:T})}\left[q_{\sigma}(\bm{x}_{T}|\bm{x}_{0})+\sum_{t=2}^{T}\log q_{\sigma}(\bm{x}_{t-1}|\bm{x}_{t},\bm{x}_{0})-\sum_{t=1}^{T}\log p_{\theta}^{(t)}(\bm{x}_{t-1}|\bm{x}_{t})-\log p_{\theta}(\bm{x}_{T})\right]$
comments.explanation: The second line of Equation (11) incorrectly expands the log-joint probability. The term $\log q_{\sigma}(\bm{x}_{1:T}|\bm{x}_{0})$ should decompose into forward-time conditionals $\sum_{t=1}^T \log q_{\sigma}(\bm{x}_t | \bm{x}_{t-1}, \bm{x}_0)$, but instead it uses $q_{\sigma}(\bm{x}_{T}|\bm{x}_{0})$ (without a logarithm) and reverse-time conditionals $\log q_{\sigma}(\bm{x}_{t-1}|\bm{x}_{t},\bm{x}_{0})$. This misrepresents the structure of the KL divergence in the variational bound. The correct expansion must follow the forward process factorization to maintain consistency with the definition of $q_{\sigma}(\bm{x}_{1:T}|\bm{x}_{0})$ in Eq. (6).

comments.title: Misattribution of determinism to forward process
comments.quote: We note another special case when  $\sigma_t = 0$  for all  $t^5$ ; the forward process becomes deterministic given  $\pmb{x}_{t-1}$  and  $\pmb{x}_0$ , except for  $t = 1$ ; in the generative process, the coefficient before the random noise  $\epsilon_t$  becomes zero.
comments.explanation: The claim that the forward process becomes deterministic when $\sigma_t = 0$ is incorrect. The parameter $\sigma_t$ appears only in the reverse process (Eq. 12) and in the non-Markovian reverse conditionals (Eq. 7), not in the forward transition $q(\bm{x}_t | \bm{x}_{t-1})$, which remains stochastic and independent of $\sigma_t$. Setting $\sigma_t = 0$ makes the reverse process deterministic, enabling DDIM's fast sampling, but does not affect the forward process. The text conflates the direction of inference and misassigns the source of determinism. This should be corrected to clarify that determinism applies to the generative (reverse) process only.

comments.title: Determinism claim overlooks model evaluation variability
comments.quote: For DDIM, the generative process is deterministic, and  $\pmb{x}_0$  would depend only on the initial state  $\pmb{x}_T$ .
comments.explanation: The assertion that the generative process is deterministic assumes that the neural network $\epsilon_\theta^{(t)}(\bm{x}_t)$ produces identical outputs for identical inputs across runs. However, in practice, model evaluation may exhibit variability due to numerical instability, quantization, or internal randomness (e.g., dropout). Even with $\sigma_t = 0$, the trajectory depends on the consistency of $\epsilon_\theta$, which is not guaranteed. The process is mathematically deterministic only if model inference is exactly reproducible. The paper should clarify that determinism holds under idealized conditions and may not be preserved in finite-precision implementations.

comments.title: Incorrect ODE reparameterization in Eq. (14)
comments.quote:
We can reparameterize $(\sqrt{1-\alpha}/\sqrt{\alpha})$ with $\lambda$ and $(\bm{x}/\sqrt{\alpha})$ with $H(\lambda)$ then sampling $\bm{x}_{0}$ with Equation (13) can be treated as integration over the following ODE:

$H(0)=\int_{M}^{0}\epsilon_{\theta}^{\lambda}(H(\lambda)\sqrt{\lambda^{2}-1})\mathrm{d}\lambda+H(M),\quad H(M)\sim\mathcal{N}(\bm{0},\bm{I})$ (14)
comments.explanation: The argument of $\epsilon_\theta^\lambda$ in Eq. (14) is $H(\lambda)\sqrt{\lambda^2 - 1}$, which does not correspond to any valid input derived from Eq. (13). For $\lambda < 1$, $\sqrt{\lambda^2 - 1}$ is imaginary, making the expression ill-defined. The correct continuous limit of Eq. (13) is $dH/d\lambda = -\epsilon_\theta(H(\lambda))$, leading to $H(0) = H(M) + \int_M^0 \epsilon_\theta^{(\lambda)}(H(\lambda)) \, d\lambda$. The current form introduces a spurious nonlinear transformation that breaks the derivation. This error undermines the ODE analogy and should be corrected.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation $\dim(\tau)$ for step count
comments.quote: we vary the number of timesteps used to generate a sample ($\dim(\tau)$) and the stochasticity of the process ($\eta$).
comments.explanation: The notation $\dim(\tau)$ is misleading: $\tau$ is a sequence of timesteps, not a vector space, so its dimension is undefined. The intended meaning is $S = |\tau|$, the length of the sequence. Using $\dim(\cdot)$ suggests a linear algebraic context and may confuse readers, especially when $\tau$ is non-uniform. Replace $\dim(\tau)$ with $|\tau|$ or $S$ throughout for clarity and consistency with standard discrete sequence notation.

comments.title: Missing justification for joint distribution form in Eq. (6)
comments.quote:
Let us consider a family $\mathcal{Q}$ of inference distributions, indexed by a real vector $\sigma\in\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}^{T}$:

$q_{\sigma}(\bm{x}_{1:T}|\bm{x}_{0}):=q_{\sigma}(\bm{x}_{T}|\bm{x}_{0})\prod_{t=2}^{T}q_{\sigma}(\bm{x}_{t-1}|\bm{x}_{t},\bm{x}_{0})$ (6)
comments.explanation: The joint distribution in Eq. (6) is defined via backward transitions conditioned on $\bm{x}_0$, which is unconventional for a forward process. While each factor is a normalized Gaussian, the paper does not verify that the product forms a valid joint distribution over $\bm{x}_{1:T}$ given $\bm{x}_0$. A brief justification should be added, noting that the construction defines a consistent Bayesian backward chain and that the product integrates to 1, ensuring it is a proper probability distribution.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces DDIM, a method to accelerate sampling in diffusion models by using non-Markovian processes. While the core idea is valuable and the empirical results are impressive, there are significant concerns about the mathematical derivations, evaluation methodology, and unexamined trade-offs that need to be addressed.

The paper presents a clever approach to accelerate diffusion model sampling with strong empirical results showing 10-50x speedups. The connection to Neural ODEs and the demonstration of latent space interpolation are particularly interesting contributions. However, the mathematical foundations contain gaps, the evaluation lacks critical ablation studies, and important limitations regarding sample diversity are not adequately acknowledged.

Overview issues:

- Mathematical gaps in non-Markovian process derivation: The derivation of the non-Markovian forward process in Section 3.1 contains critical gaps. Equation (7) defines q_σ(x_{t-1}|x_t, x_0) but does not prove that this leads to the desired marginal q_σ(x_t|x_0) for all t. Lemma 1 is referenced but not shown in the main text, making it impossible to verify the claim. More importantly, the transition from Eq. (6) to the variational objective J_σ in Eq. (11) assumes the existence of a valid reverse process without establishing its mathematical soundness. Theorem 1 claims equivalence between J_σ and L_γ but provides no proof sketch, leaving readers to accept a fundamental claim without justification. This undermines the theoretical foundation of the entire approach.

- Unexamined trade-off between speed and sample diversity: The paper emphasizes DDIM's speed improvements but fails to properly investigate the impact on sample diversity. The deterministic sampling (η=0) that enables speedup necessarily reduces stochastic exploration of the sample space. While Figure 5 shows 'consistency' as a benefit, this could actually indicate mode collapse. The paper should include quantitative diversity metrics (e.g., precision/recall for generative models, or simply count of unique samples) to demonstrate that the accelerated sampling does not sacrifice coverage of the data distribution. The literature context mentions this as a known limitation, yet the paper presents it only as a positive consistency property without acknowledging the potential downside.

- Inadequate ablation of η parameter and variance schedules: The experiments in Table 1 vary η but do not provide sufficient analysis of its impact. The paper introduces η as a hyperparameter controlling stochasticity but only tests a few discrete values (0, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0). There is no investigation of optimal η values for different step counts or datasets, nor any discussion of how η should be chosen in practice. Additionally, the comparison with the σ schedule from Ho et al. (2020) shows dramatically worse performance at small step counts, but the paper does not explain why this occurs or how to mitigate it. A proper ablation should include: (1) a sweep of η values with finer granularity, (2) analysis of optimal η vs. step count relationship, and (3) investigation of alternative variance schedules beyond the one in Eq. (15).

- Missing baseline comparisons with accelerated sampling methods: The paper compares DDIM against vanilla DDPM but fails to compare against other accelerated sampling methods for diffusion models that existed at the time. The literature context mentions that this is an active research area, yet no comparisons are included against methods like knowledge distillation for diffusion models or other techniques for reducing sampling steps. This makes it difficult to assess whether DDIM's improvements are truly novel or simply incremental over existing acceleration techniques. At minimum, the paper should include comparisons with: (1) DDPM with fewer training steps (not just sampling steps), (2) any published acceleration methods from 2020, and (3) a discussion of how DDIM's approach differs fundamentally from these alternatives.

- Unclear relationship between training steps and sampling steps: Section 4.2 suggests that models can be trained with T=1000 steps but sampled with far fewer steps, but the relationship between training trajectory length and sampling trajectory length is not explored. The paper claims 'no changes are needed with regards to the training procedure' but provides no evidence that a model trained on 1000 steps can generalize to sampling trajectories of arbitrary length. There should be experiments showing: (1) performance when training and sampling with matched step counts, (2) performance when training with fewer steps (e.g., 100) and sampling with even fewer, and (3) analysis of whether there's an optimal ratio between training and sampling steps. Without this, it's unclear whether the impressive results require the expensive 1000-step training or could be achieved more efficiently.

- Missing demonstration of non-vacuous result: The paper presents DDIM as a generalization of DDPM but never demonstrates that this generalization is non-vacuous. A complete paper should include a concrete example where the non-Markovian forward process leads to a different generative process than the standard Markovian DDPM. Specifically, the authors should compute the forward process q_σ and reverse process p_θ for a simple 2D Gaussian mixture model with T=4, showing how different σ values (e.g., σ=0 for DDIM vs. Markovian σ) produce different sampling trajectories. This would verify that the claimed generalization actually enables new behaviors rather than just being a mathematical reformulation.

- No worked parametric example: The theoretical derivations remain abstract without a concrete, fully-specified example. The paper should include a detailed computation for a standard model from the field, such as a 2D Swiss roll dataset with T=10 steps, showing: (1) the exact form of q_σ(x_t|x_0) for both Markovian and non-Markovian cases, (2) the corresponding reverse process equations, and (3) actual sample trajectories plotted for different η values. This would help readers build intuition about how the non-Markovian process works in practice and verify that the mathematics translates to observable differences in sampling behavior.

- Underdeveloped interpolation implications: The paper claims DDIM enables 'semantically meaningful image interpolation' but only shows qualitative examples in Figure 6. This central claim needs quantitative development. The authors should include: (1) interpolation experiments on a dataset with semantic structure (e.g., MNIST digit classes or CelebA attributes), (2) metrics measuring smoothness of interpolation (e.g., trajectory length in feature space or classifier confidence along interpolation path), and (3) comparison against baseline interpolation methods including both DDPM and GAN latent interpolation. Without this, the interpolation claim remains anecdotal rather than a demonstrated advantage.

- Missing practical implementation guidance: The paper provides no discussion of how to estimate key quantities in practice, particularly for the accelerated sampling in Section 4.2. When sampling with τ << T, the paper claims 'only slight changes to updates in Eq. (12) are needed' but doesn't specify: (1) how to handle the α_t values for skipped timesteps, (2) whether the same ε_θ^(t) network can be used for all τ values or requires adaptation, and (3) practical considerations for choosing τ given computational constraints. A complete paper should include implementation details and possibly pseudocode for the accelerated sampling procedure, as this is essential for reproducibility and practical adoption.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide complete mathematical proofs for Theorem 1 and Lemma 1 in the main text or appendix

- Add quantitative evaluation of sample diversity using established metrics to address potential mode collapse

- Include comprehensive ablation study of η parameter and alternative variance schedules with finer granularity

- Add comparisons with existing accelerated sampling methods for diffusion models as baselines

- Investigate the relationship between training steps and sampling steps through controlled experiments

comments.title: Missing proof for Theorem 1 equivalence
comments.quote: For all $\sigma>\bm{0}$, there exists $\gamma\in\mathbb{R}_{>0}^{T}$ and $C\in\mathbb{R}$, such that $J_{\sigma}=L_{\gamma}+C$.
comments.explanation: Theorem 1 claims equivalence between J_σ and L_γ but provides no proof. The paper uses this theorem to justify that optimal solutions are invariant to σ, yet without showing the mathematical relationship between σ and γ, readers cannot verify this central claim. The equivalence is stated without any derivation, proof sketch, or reference to supporting material. Add a proof showing how γ and C are constructed from σ, or reference where this proof appears.

comments.title: Undefined L_γ objective function
comments.quote: However, $J_{\sigma}$ is equivalent to $L_{\gamma}$ for certain weights $\gamma$, as we show below.
comments.explanation: The paper references L_γ throughout Theorem 1 and subsequent discussion but never defines this objective function. Without knowing what L_γ looks like or how the weights γ affect it, readers cannot understand the claimed equivalence or verify the properties attributed to L_γ. Add the formal definition of L_γ before Theorem 1, showing how it relates to the weighted sum of terms mentioned in the discussion.

comments.title: Unverified marginal distribution claim
comments.quote: The mean function is chosen to order to ensure that $q_{\sigma}(\bm{x}_{t}|\bm{x}_{0})=\mathcal{N}(\sqrt{\alpha_{t}}\bm{x}_{0},(1-\alpha_{t})\bm{I})$ for all $t$ (see Lemma 1 of Appendix B), so that it defines a joint inference distribution that matches the “marginals” as desired.
comments.explanation: The paper makes a critical claim about marginal distributions without verification in the main text. The claim that the specific mean function in Eq. (7) ensures $q_{\sigma}(\bm{x}_{t}|\bm{x}_{0})=\mathcal{N}(\sqrt{\alpha_{t}}\bm{x}_{0},(1-\alpha_{t})\bm{I})$ is fundamental to the entire non-Markovian framework. Since this property is essential for the paper's contributions and the proof is relegated to an appendix, readers cannot verify the core claim without leaving the main text. Add a brief sketch of the proof after the quoted text: 'We can verify this by induction: for $t=1$, the result holds by definition. Assuming it holds for time $t$, the conditional mean in Eq. (7) is chosen such that $\mathbb{E}[\bm{x}_{t-1}|\bm{x}_{t},\bm{x}_{0}] = \sqrt{\alpha_{t-1}}\bm{x}_{0}$ and $\text{Var}(\bm{x}_{t-1}|\bm{x}_{t},\bm{x}_{0}) = 1-\alpha_{t-1}$, which preserves the marginal form at time $t-1$.'

comments.title: Missing justification for unchanged training with subsampling
comments.quote: Using a similar argument as in Section 3, we can justify using the model trained with the  $L_{1}$  objective, so no changes are needed in training. We show that only slight changes to the updates in Eq. (12) are needed to obtain the new, faster generative processes, which applies to DDPM, DDIM, as well as all generative processes considered in Eq. (10).
comments.explanation: The claim that training remains unchanged when sampling from a subset of timesteps requires explicit justification. While the marginal q_σ(x_t|x_0) at sampled timesteps remains the same, the variational bound derivation in Section 3 depends on the full Markov chain structure. When using only a subset {τ₁,...,τ_S}, the reverse process operates over fewer steps, potentially changing the optimal parameters for the L₁ objective. The paper should explicitly derive the variational bound for the reduced reverse process or cite a theorem showing the L₁ objective remains optimal under subsampling. Rewrite the sentence as 'Using the same L₁ objective remains optimal because the marginal distributions q_σ(x_τ_i|x_0) are unchanged, as shown in Appendix C.1' and provide the detailed derivation.

comments.title: Logical inconsistency about parameter encoding
comments.quote: minor details that affects sample quality are encoded in the parameters, as longer sample trajectories give better quality samples but do not significantly affect the high-level features.
comments.explanation: This statement contains a logical inconsistency. The network parameters θ are fixed during sampling and cannot encode different information for different trajectory lengths. If longer trajectories produce better quality samples, this improvement must come from the sampling process itself (e.g., more accurate intermediate computations), not from additional information being 'encoded in the parameters'. The parameters are the same regardless of whether you use 20 or 1000 steps. This suggests a misunderstanding of how the sampling process works. The authors should clarify that quality improvements come from more accurate intermediate state estimation, not from parameter encoding.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about DDPM interpolation limitations
comments.quote: This is different from the interpolation procedure in Ho et al. (2020), since in DDPM the same  $\boldsymbol{x}_T$  would lead to highly diverse  $\boldsymbol{x}_0$  due to the stochastic generative process.
comments.explanation: The claim that DDPM cannot perform meaningful interpolation because the same x_T leads to diverse x_0 is not accurate. In DDPM, one can perform deterministic interpolation by fixing the random seeds during the reverse diffusion process, which would produce consistent x_0 from the same x_T. The paper should clarify that they are referring to the standard stochastic sampling procedure, or acknowledge that deterministic interpolation is possible in DDPM with fixed randomness. Rewrite as: "This differs from the standard stochastic interpolation in DDPM, where the same \boldsymbol{x}_T typically leads to diverse \boldsymbol{x}_0 due to the stochastic generative process, though deterministic interpolation is possible with fixed random seeds."

comments.title: Unspecified condition for trajectory validity
comments.quote: Let us consider the forward process as defined not on all the latent variables  $\pmb{x}_{1:T}$ , but on a subset  $\{\pmb{x}_{\tau_1},\dots,\pmb{x}_{\tau_S}\}$ , where  $\tau$  is an increasing sub-sequence of  $[1,\dots,T]$  of length  $S$ . In particular, we define the sequential forward process over  $\pmb{x}_{\tau_1},\dots,\pmb{x}_{\tau_S}$  such that  $q(\pmb{x}_{\tau_i}|\pmb{x}_0) = \mathcal{N}(\sqrt{\alpha_{\tau_i}}\pmb{x}_0,(1 - \alpha_{\tau_i})\pmb{I})$  matches the "margins"
comments.explanation: The paper assumes that any increasing subsequence τ yields a valid sequential forward process, but this requires the Markov property to hold between consecutive τ_i steps. For arbitrary τ, the transition q(x_{τ_i}|x_{τ_{i-1}}) may not be Gaussian or may have different parameters than implied by the marginal specification. The construction only works if the forward process satisfies the semigroup property q(x_{τ_i}|x_{τ_{i-1}}) = N(√(α_{τ_i}/α_{τ_{i-1}})x_{τ_{i-1}}, (1-α_{τ_i}/α_{τ_{i-1}})I). Add 'This construction requires that the forward process satisfies the semigroup property, which holds for the variance schedule in Eq. (6)' after the quoted sentence.

comments.title: Undefined parameter constraints
comments.quote:
where $q_{\sigma}(\bm{x}_{T}|\bm{x}_{0})=\mathcal{N}(\sqrt{\alpha_{T}}\bm{x}_{0},(1-\alpha_{T})\bm{I})$ and for all $t>1$,

$q_{\sigma}(\bm{x}_{t-1}|\bm{x}_{t},\bm{x}_{0})=\mathcal{N}\left(\sqrt{\alpha_{t-1}}\bm{x}_{0}+\sqrt{1-\alpha_{t-1}-\sigma_{t}^{2}}\cdot\frac{\bm{x}_{t}-\sqrt{\alpha_{t}}\bm{x}_{0}}{\sqrt{1-\alpha_{t}}},\sigma_{t}^{2}\bm{I}\right).$ (7)
comments.explanation: The paper does not specify the necessary constraints on the parameters α_t and σ_t for the expressions to be valid. In Eq. (7), the term √(1-α_{t-1}-σ_t²) requires 1-α_{t-1}-σ_t² ≥ 0 to be real-valued, and √(1-α_t) in the denominator requires 1-α_t > 0. Without these constraints, the expressions may be undefined or complex-valued. Add after Eq. (7): 'We assume 0 ≤ α_t < 1 for all t and σ_t² ≤ 1-α_{t-1} to ensure all square roots are real-valued and denominators are non-zero.'

comments.title: Table formatting error in reconstruction results
comments.explanation:
The table formatting appears to have the step values and error values in the wrong columns. The header row shows 'S' followed by the step values (10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1000), but the data row shows 'Error' followed by what should be the error values (0.014, 0.0065, etc.). This creates a mismatch where the step values are treated as column headers rather than data points. The table should be reformatted with 'S' as the first column header and the step values in the first column, followed by 'Error' as the second column header with the corresponding error values. Rewrite the table as:

| S | Error |
|---|---|
| 10 | 0.014 |
| 20 | 0.0065 |
| 50 | 0.0023 |
| 100 | 0.0009 |
| 200 | 0.0004 |
| 500 | 0.0001 |
| 1000 | 0.0001 |

comments.title: Contradictory quality assessment of DDIM
comments.quote: We observe that DDIM ($\eta=0$) achieves the best sample quality when $\dim(\tau)$ is small, and DDPM ($\eta=1$ and $\hat{\sigma}$) typically has worse sample quality compared to its less stochastic counterparts with the same $\dim(\tau)$, except for the case for $\dim(\tau)=1000$ and $\hat{\sigma}$ reported by *Ho et al. (2020)* where DDIM is marginally worse.
comments.explanation: The text claims DDIM is 'marginally worse' than DDPM with σ̂ at 1000 steps, but Table 1 shows DDIM (η=0.0) achieves FID=4.04 on CIFAR10 and FID=3.51 on CelebA, while σ̂ achieves FID=3.17 and FID=3.26 respectively. The differences (0.87 for CIFAR10, 0.25 for CelebA) are not marginal - DDPM with σ̂ is substantially better, especially on CIFAR10. The claim should be corrected to reflect that DDPM with σ̂ significantly outperforms DDIM at full 1000-step sampling.

comments.title: Inconsistent σ notation definition
comments.quote:
comparisons, we consider $\sigma$ with the form:

$\sigma_{\tau_{i}}(\eta)=\eta\sqrt{(1-\alpha_{\tau_{i-1}})/(1-\alpha_{\tau_{i}})}\sqrt{1-\alpha_{\tau_{i}}/\alpha_{\tau_{i-1}}},$ (15)

where $\eta\in\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ is a hyperparameter that we can directly control. This includes an original DDPM generative process when $\eta=1$ and DDIM when $\eta=0$. We also consider DDPM where the random noise has a larger standard deviation than $\sigma(1)$, which we denote as $\hat{\sigma}$: $\hat{\sigma}_{\tau_{i}}=\sqrt{1-\alpha_{\tau_{i}}/\alpha_{\tau_{i-1}}}$ .
comments.explanation: The notation for σ is inconsistent within the same paragraph. Equation (15) defines σ as a function of η, but the text then refers to σ(1) without the τ_i subscript, which is undefined. The comparison to σ(1) should be to σ_{τ_i}(1) to maintain consistency with the notation established in Equation (15). This creates ambiguity about whether σ(1) refers to a different quantity or is simply a notational shorthand. Rewrite the comparison as 'larger standard deviation than σ_{τ_i}(1)' to maintain consistent notation throughout the paragraph.

comments.title: Missing footnote reference
comments.quote: of Eq. (14)) $^7$ . We consider encoding and decoding on the CIFAR-10 test set
comments.explanation: The text includes a footnote marker '$^7$' but there is no corresponding footnote provided in this section. This creates an incomplete reference that readers cannot follow. Either remove the footnote marker or add the missing footnote content that explains the methodological detail referenced by the superscript 7.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation in interpolation dimension
comments.quote: Figure 6: Interpolation of samples from DDIM with  $\dim (\tau) = 50$
comments.explanation: The notation \dim(τ) = 50 is unclear. If τ represents a timestep sequence, \dim(τ) would typically be the length of the sequence (50 steps), but this should be explicitly defined. The paper should clarify whether this means 50 interpolation steps, 50 diffusion timesteps, or something else. Add a clarification: "Figure 6: Interpolation of samples from DDIM using 50 timesteps (|τ| = 50)"

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces DDIM, a deterministic variant of diffusion models that accelerates sampling by decoupling the training objective from the Markovian assumption of the forward process. While the empirical speedups and latent-space properties are compelling, the theoretical justification for few-step sampling, the treatment of diversity loss, and the lack of likelihood evaluation leave key probabilistic claims unverified.

DDIM provides an elegant reformulation of diffusion models that bridges variational inference and implicit generative modeling. The empirical results convincingly demonstrate significant sampling acceleration and novel capabilities like latent interpolation. However, the paper would benefit from stronger theoretical grounding regarding discretization error, diversity trade-offs, and probabilistic guarantees.

Overview issues:

- Missing discretization error analysis for accelerated sampling: Section 4.2 claims that sampling along a shortened trajectory $\tau$ with $S \ll T$ steps preserves sample quality, yet the paper offers no theoretical bound on the approximation error introduced by skipping intermediate steps. The authors note the similarity to Euler integration in Section 4.3 and Equation 13, but they never formalize the continuous limit or analyze how the discretization error scales with step count. Without an error analysis or step-size sensitivity study, readers cannot assess whether the observed quality retention is a property of the model or merely an artifact of the specific datasets and schedules used. Adding a formal derivation of the continuous-time limit or including a systematic study of numerical error versus step count would strengthen the methodological foundation.

- Unquantified trade-off between consistency and sample diversity: The deterministic setting $\eta=0$ eliminates stochasticity from the reverse process, which the paper frames as a benefit for consistency and interpolation in Section 5.2. This shift, however, inherently reduces sample diversity and can exacerbate mode coverage issues common to implicit models. The authors do not report any metrics to quantify this trade-off, such as sample entropy, coverage ratios, or FID-k, leaving the practical impact of deterministic sampling unclear. Readers will wonder whether the speedup comes at the cost of generating repetitive or narrow distributions. The revision should include diversity metrics alongside FID and explicitly discuss how $\eta$ controls the consistency-diversity trade-off.

- Absence of likelihood evaluation and variational gap analysis: The paper trains DDIM using the variational lower bound in Equation 2 and Theorem 1, but DDIM is explicitly described as an implicit probabilistic model in Section 4.1. Implicit models with deterministic reverse processes typically lack a tractable likelihood, and the paper never evaluates likelihood or discusses how the variational bound behaves for non-Markovian forward processes. This omission leaves the probabilistic guarantees of the method undefined and makes it difficult to compare DDIM against likelihood-based baselines. The authors should either report likelihood estimates, discuss the theoretical gap between the variational bound and true likelihood in the non-Markovian regime, or clearly position DDIM as a likelihood-free generative model.

- Inconsistent full-step performance contradicts superiority claims: Table 1 shows that DDIM with $\eta=0$ achieves an FID of 4.04 on CIFAR10 at $S=1000$, while the DDPM variant using $\hat{\sigma}$ reaches 3.17 under the same conditions. The authors describe this as marginally worse but do not investigate why the deterministic variant underperforms the fully stochastic one when all steps are used. This result directly challenges the claim that DDIM consistently outperforms DDPM or matches it without compromise. The discrepancy likely stems from the deterministic update ignoring beneficial stochastic fluctuations that help navigate complex energy landscapes at full resolution. The revision should analyze the mechanism behind this performance gap and adjust the claims to reflect that deterministic sampling is advantageous primarily in the few-step regime.

- Heuristic ODE connection lacks numerical stability discussion: Section 4.3 reparameterizes the update rule to resemble an ordinary differential equation and suggests using the reverse process for encoding in Section 5.4. The derivation, however, remains heuristic and does not address numerical stability, boundary conditions, or solver sensitivity. Table 2 demonstrates that reconstruction error decreases with more steps, but the authors do not discuss whether adaptive step-size strategies or higher-order solvers could improve accuracy or stability. Without this analysis, the claim that DDIM behaves like a Neural ODE remains unverified and potentially misleading for practitioners attempting to use the reverse process for inversion tasks. The authors should formalize the continuous limit, test alternative ODE solvers, or clarify the practical limitations of the encoding procedure.

- Analytical derivation for a low-dimensional Gaussian model: The paper develops a general theory for non-Markovian forward processes and deterministic reverse sampling in high-dimensional image spaces, but never computes these quantities for a simple parametric model. Readers cannot easily verify the consistency property or understand how the variance parameter $σ$ alters the reverse trajectory without a concrete example. The authors should compute the reverse update rule (Eq. 12) and the resulting trajectory for a 1D or 2D Gaussian forward process with a linear noise schedule. This worked example would demonstrate analytically how $σ=0$ collapses the stochastic DDPM path into a deterministic curve, and how the initial latent $bx_T$ uniquely determines the output. This analytical grounding would help readers calibrate their expectations for high-dimensional results and clarify the exact mechanism behind the claimed consistency property.

- Guidance on selecting the sampling trajectory $τ$: Section 4.2 introduces accelerated generation by sampling along a subsequence $τ$ of length $S << T$, but the paper provides no theoretical or empirical guidance on how to choose the spacing of these steps. The authors use uniform spacing in their experiments, yet the discretization error of the reverse process likely depends on the local curvature of the score function, which varies with the noise schedule $α_t$. Without recommendations on step placement, practitioners may struggle to replicate the reported quality-efficiency trade-off. Readers will wonder whether uniform spacing is optimal or if adaptive strategies yield better fidelity per step. Providing this implementation guidance bridges the gap between the theoretical framework and practical deployment.

- Analysis of latent space geometry and downstream utility: The paper claims that DDIM enables semantically meaningful interpolation and reconstruction, positioning the model as a practical alternative to VAEs and normalizing flows for latent manipulation. However, the authors do not analyze the geometry of the learned latent space $bx_T$. They report reconstruction error but omit metrics that would clarify whether the latent representation is continuous or suitable for downstream tasks like attribute editing or transfer learning. Without this analysis, the downstream utility remains an unverified claim rather than a demonstrated capability. The revision should include these diagnostics to substantiate the paper's positioning of DDIM as a versatile generative tool.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add a theoretical or empirical analysis of discretization error and step-size sensitivity for accelerated sampling trajectories.

- Include diversity metrics (e.g., sample entropy, coverage, or FID-k) to quantify the consistency-diversity trade-off controlled by $\eta$.

- Report likelihood estimates or explicitly discuss the variational gap for non-Markovian forward processes to clarify probabilistic guarantees.

- Investigate and explain the performance gap between deterministic DDIM and stochastic DDPM at full step counts, adjusting claims accordingly.

- Formalize the ODE connection, test alternative numerical solvers, and discuss stability limitations of the reverse encoding process.

comments.title: Algebraic error in continuous ODE derivation
comments.quote: $H(0)=\int_{M}^{0}\epsilon_{\theta}^{\lambda}(H(\lambda)\sqrt{\lambda^{2}-1})\mathrm{d}\lambda+H(M),\quad H(M)\sim\mathcal{N}(\bm{0},\bm{I})$
comments.explanation: The transition from the discrete update in Eq. (13) to the continuous ODE contains a scaling factor mistake. Using the definition $\lambda = \sqrt{(1-\alpha)/\alpha}$, we get $\alpha = 1/(1+\lambda^2)$. Since $H(\lambda) = \bm{x}/\sqrt{\alpha}$, it follows that $\bm{x} = H(\lambda)/\sqrt{1+\lambda^2}$. Substituting this into the continuous limit of Eq. (13) yields the correct ODE $dH/d\lambda = \epsilon_{\theta}^{\lambda}(H(\lambda)/\sqrt{1+\lambda^2})$. The term $\sqrt{\lambda^2-1}$ in the paper's Eq. (14) is mathematically invalid for $\lambda < 1$ (which corresponds to $\alpha > 0.5$) and appears to be a typographical error. Please correct the scaling factor to $1/\sqrt{1+\lambda^2}$ to match the algebraic relationship between $\lambda$ and $\alpha$.

comments.title: Incorrect characterization of NCSN density estimation
comments.quote: DDPMs optimize a variational lower bound to the log-likelihood, whereas NCSNs optimize the score matching objective (Hyvärinen, 2005) over a nonparametric Parzen density estimator of the data (Vincent, 2011; Raphan & Simoncelli, 2011).
comments.explanation: The claim that NCSNs optimize score matching over a Parzen density estimator is factually incorrect. Score matching, as introduced by Hyvärinen (2005), is specifically designed to bypass explicit density estimation by matching the score function directly. Song & Ermon (2019) employ denoising score matching, which learns a neural network to approximate this score function without ever constructing a Parzen density or estimating the partition function. Citing Vincent (2011) and Raphan & Simoncelli (2011) in this context conflates score matching with kernel density estimation. Please clarify that NCSNs operate directly on the score function, entirely avoiding the intractable normalization constant and explicit density estimation.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Langevin dynamics and gradient flow
comments.quote: Since Langevin dynamics is a discretization of a gradient flow (Jordan et al., 1998), both DDPM and NCSN require many steps to achieve good sample quality.
comments.explanation: This statement mischaracterizes both Langevin dynamics and the cited theoretical work. Jordan et al. (1998) describes the gradient flow as the continuous-time limit of deterministic optimization in the Wasserstein space, not Langevin dynamics. Langevin dynamics inherently includes a stochastic noise term and corresponds to a stochastic differential equation, not a deterministic gradient flow. Furthermore, DDPM sampling is derived from a reverse-time diffusion process that approximates an SDE or ODE, rather than a gradient flow. The requirement for many sampling steps stems from discretization errors in approximating the reverse diffusion trajectory, not from a gradient flow interpretation. Please revise this explanation to accurately reflect the stochastic nature of diffusion sampling.

comments.title: Misattribution of DDIM's deterministic property to variational derivation
comments.quote: We derive DDIM from a purely variational perspective, where the restrictions of Langevin dynamics are not relevant; this could partially explain why we are able to observe superior sample quality compared to DDPM under fewer iterations.
comments.explanation: This claim misrepresents the theoretical foundation of DDIM. The deterministic reverse process in DDIM is derived from assuming a non-Markovian forward diffusion process, not from a purely variational perspective. While the training objective is indeed a variational lower bound, the key innovation enabling few-step sampling is the non-Markovian assumption that decouples the reverse transition from the immediate previous state. The variational bound alone does not remove the restrictions of Langevin dynamics or guarantee deterministic trajectories. Please clarify that the non-Markovian forward process is the primary driver of the deterministic sampling property, rather than attributing it solely to the variational formulation.
